intro: The clearest difference between Cody and Chartboost is pricing — Cody is freemium (has a free tier), while Chartboost is subscription (no free tier). Beyond that, lock-in — Cody has low lock-in (Sourcegraph AI, OSS core), whereas Chartboost has medium lock-in (Proprietary mobile ad SDK). The stated fit for both tools is similar on paper: Cody is pitched as Code Assistants / Agent Tooling tool for developers. Specializes in AI Code Search, while Chartboost is pitched as mobile ad network and monetization SDK for in-app advertising with programmatic auction support. On the strengths side, Cody is cited for accelerating coding workflow, while Chartboost is cited for code generation and refactoring. The honest trade-off: both Cody and Chartboost are the wrong call for non-coding roles. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap. On developer experience the data shows Cody at 5/5 and Chartboost at 3/5, with transparency at 4/5 and 3/5 respectively.
quick_take: Cody and Chartboost both target Code Assistants / Agent Tooling; their data-level fit descriptions overlap, so decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Cody if you need a Code Assistants / Agent Tooling tool and a freemium (has a free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than subscription (no free tier).
verdict_b: Choose Chartboost if you need a Code Assistants / Agent Tooling tool and a subscription (no free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than freemium (has a free tier).

faq.a: Cody is freemium with a free tier, while Chartboost is subscription, so the cheaper option depends on your usage shape. Listed pricing pages usually change, so check both directly before committing. For small teams, the free-tier limits often matter more than headline prices.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Cody to Chartboost?
faq.a: Migrating from Cody to Chartboost is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. Cody has low lock-in, so exporting state is typically straightforward. Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

On our developer-experience score, Cody is rated 5/5 and Chartboost 3/5, so Cody edges ahead. Scores like this smooth over real differences in docs, CLI feel, and SDK quality, so treat them as a starting point. Spend an afternoon in each before relying on the number.

faq.q: Is Chartboost a good alternative to Cody?
faq.a: Chartboost is a reasonable alternative to Cody when Chartboost fits accelerating coding workflow. If your use case matches Cody's sweet spot — accelerating coding workflow — staying with Cody is probably safer. Cody itself is not meant for non-coding roles, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
